metadata.seed: CoVid has created a situation where I have to work from home all the time and I am unsure whether I will be employed next month

patient.query: CoVid has created a situation where I have to work from home all the time and I am unsure whether I will be employed next month

automatic_thoughts: I might lose my job
I can't handle this situation

llm_response.response: It sounds like the uncertainty about your job is weighing heavily on you. What evidence do you have that suggests you might lose your position, and what evidence might point the other way? How might you view this situation if you were advising a friend in the same spot?

patient.query: Yeah, it really does feel like a heavy cloud hanging over me. I mean, there have been some hints, like the budget cuts and hearing rumors about layoffs, but I’ve also had some good feedback from my boss lately. If I were to advise a friend, I’d probably say to focus on what they’ve done well, but… it’s hard to take my own advice right now. It just feels like I’m waiting for the other shoe to drop.

automatic_thoughts: waiting for the other shoe to drop
hard to take my own advice

neutral: Loss of hope for the future (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re carrying a heavy weight right now, with the budget cuts and rumors adding to that sense of looming uncertainty. Even though you’ve received positive feedback, it can be hard to feel confident in your own strengths when the future feels so uncertain. It’s understandable to feel like you’re waiting for the other shoe to drop, and that anxiety can feel overwhelming.

patient.query: Yeah, it really is overwhelming sometimes. I keep trying to remind myself of the good things, like the positive feedback, but then the worries creep in. It’s almost like they drown out the good moments. I just can’t shake this feeling that it’s not going to last, you know? Like I’ll be left in the dark again.

automatic_thoughts: the good moments won't last
I'll be left in the dark again

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling overwhelmed by worries that the good moments won’t last and that you’ll end up alone again. What evidence do you have that the positive experiences might actually be lasting? Are there moments when you’ve felt that the good times stayed with you?

retrieved_concepts_used.concept: negative belief about lasting positive moments
retrieved_concepts_used.label: unknown

patient.query: I guess there have been times when things felt stable, like when I got that promotion last year. But even that feels like a long time ago, like everything’s changed since then. I just can’t help but think that I can be on top of the world one minute and then it all falls apart the next. It’s almost like I just expect things to turn sour, especially when I start feeling happy or secure.

automatic_thoughts: everything’s changed since then
I can be on top of the world one minute and then it all falls apart the next
I just expect things to turn sour

neutral: Loss of hope for the future (finding)
contradiction: Tends to be stable in affect (finding)

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing a pattern where moments of stability feel fleeting, and that can be unsettling. What evidence do you have that things are truly falling apart, and are there times when they’ve stayed steady longer than you expect? Exploring those moments might help you see a more balanced view of how your experiences unfold.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I do see that pattern. It’s just hard to focus on the times when things were steady because the anxiety seems to take over. I keep thinking of all the times things went wrong—I can’t shake those memories. But, um, now that you mention it, there have been days or even weeks when everything felt okay, and I didn’t have to worry so much. I just wish I could hold onto that feeling longer.

automatic_thoughts: I can't shake those memories
I wish I could hold onto that feeling longer

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing how anxiety pulls you back into those memories, even when things were going well. What do you think might help you keep that sense of steadiness for longer? And what was different about those days when you felt okay?

patient.query: Yeah, that does make sense. I mean, I guess on those days when I felt better, I was more focused on what I was doing, and I didn’t let the little things get to me as much. I was more in the moment, you know? But when I’m feeling anxious, it’s like I can’t see past the what-ifs. Maybe I need to find a way to just let go of that fear, but... I don’t really know how. It feels

automatic_thoughts: I can’t see past the what-ifs
maybe I need to find a way to let go of that fear
behaviors: not letting little things get to me
being more focused on what I was doing

llm_response.response: It sounds like the what‑ifs feel especially heavy when you’re anxious. What kinds of thoughts or images come up when you notice those what‑ifs? Have you seen any moments where you were able to step back from them, even briefly? What might a supportive friend suggest you do in those moments?

patient.query: Yeah, that does make sense. I mean, on those days when I felt better, I was more focused on what I was doing, and I didn’t let the little things get to me as much. But when I’m feeling anxious, it’s like I can’t see past the what-ifs. I guess I just wish I could hold onto that feeling longer. It feels like the fear just creeps back in and takes over.

automatic_thoughts: I can’t see past the what-ifs
I wish I could hold onto that feeling longer
the fear just creeps back in and takes over

llm_response.response: It sounds like you notice a clear shift between those days when you feel more focused and the moments when anxiety and those “what‑ifs” start to take over. That contrast can feel frustrating, especially when you wish you could hold onto the calm longer. What might help you keep that sense of focus when the anxiety begins to creep back in?

patient.query: Yeah, it’s really frustrating. I think I used to have some little routines that helped me stay grounded, but lately, I just forget to do them or feel too overwhelmed to even start. Like maybe taking breaks during the day or just stepping outside for a bit. I think I also hesitate because sometimes I feel like they won’t help anyway, so why bother? It’s like I’m caught in this cycle of wanting to feel better but not really knowing how to get there.

automatic_thoughts: they won’t help anyway
why bother?

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re feeling stuck between wanting to feel better and doubting whether a routine will help. Have you noticed any small moments—maybe a brief walk or a short break—where you felt a bit more relaxed? Even tiny shifts can add up, and exploring those moments might give you a clearer sense of what works for you.

patient.query: Yeah, I guess there have been a few moments. Like when I actually took a short walk last week, I did feel a bit clearer in my head. But then I think about how rare those moments are, and it’s like they don’t count as much. It’s just hard not to feel like I’m stuck in this loop of worry. I mean, I want to change how I feel, but it feels like every time I try, I just end up back in the same place

automatic_thoughts: those moments don't count as much
I'm stuck in this loop of worry
every time I try, I end up back in the same place
behaviors: trying to change how I feel

llm_response.response: It sounds like you’re noticing those clearer moments feel fleeting and maybe not enough to change your overall pattern. What evidence do you have that those moments don’t actually matter, or that they’re not contributing to a gradual shift? How might you view them as small steps rather than isolated events?

patient.query: Yeah, I guess I keep thinking that because they don’t last long, they don’t really count for much. It’s like I’m just waiting for the next wave of anxiety to hit, so I don’t give those moments enough credit. Maybe they are small steps, but it’s hard to see it that way when I’m feeling so stuck. I mean, deep down, I want to believe they could lead to something better, but it’s just… so hard to trust that.

automatic_thoughts: they don’t last long, they don’t really count for much
I’m just waiting for the next wave of anxiety to hit
it’s hard to see it that way when I’m feeling so stuck
deep down, I want to believe they could lead to something better
it’s just so hard to trust that

llm_response.response: I hear you feeling stuck and doubting the value of those brief moments. What evidence do you have that a short experience can’t have lasting meaning? How might you notice or remember a time when a small step actually led to something positive?
